what_it_does: This file is a JSON catalogue that adds an interpretive, meta-level layer over a set of design substrates (pitch decks and landing-page mockups). For each artefact it records a depth/compression level, a factual description, a narrative-arc summary, and a per-slide or per-zone attention-flow account.
observations: Contains seven entries covering capital-raise part 1, capital-raise part 2, company-info, deck1-2026, pitch-deck, presentation-15p, and product-vi-landing.
Each entry follows a consistent schema: file, collection, depth_level, what_it_is, narrative_arc, attention_flow.
Attention-flow entries describe eye-entry points, scan sequences, and focal anchors using cinematic language (e.g., 'Eye enters...', 'lands on...', 'reads L→R').
The capital-raise-1 entry treats the 15 slide titles as one grammatically continuous sentence held together by connectives.
deck1-2026 distinguishes two visual modes: immersive chrome (full-bleed renders) and content chrome (structured near-white slides).
product-vi-landing covers a one-pager plus three audience-specific landing-page mockups (parent, Developers, Architects).
The last attention_flow array is truncated in the provided file content.
standouts: The 15 titles of capital-raise-1 are authored as a single continuous sentence, with each post-cover title beginning with a connective that links to the prior slide.
deck1-2026 explicitly alternates immersive and content chrome modes as grammatical bookends to the argument.
product-vi-landing maps the same landing-page skeleton across two audience segments (Developers and Architects) with reframed copy and components.
conventions: JSON array of objects with a fixed, predictable field set.
Neutral, descriptive tone; no evaluative ranking of artefacts.
Uses directional arrows and connective language to model visual and narrative flow.
Labels each artefact with a depth_level that signals compression (1-page, 15-page, 18-page, 30-page).
concerns: The attention-flow claims are interpretive and not obviously grounded in empirical eye-tracking data.
The product-vi-landing attention_flow entry is cut off, leaving its per-zone descriptions incomplete.
Some descriptions are highly subjective (e.g., 'the most surprising line in the entire deck') without stated source or method.
notes: A meta-annotation ledger that turns pitch decks and landing pages into a retrievable index of narrative arcs and eye-flow choreography.
questions: What method or source produced the attention-flow sequences?
How is depth_level assigned, and what distinguishes '30-page expanded' from '15-page' beyond slide count?
Is the truncated product-vi-landing entry complete in the original source, or is the cut-off intentional?
purpose_vs_actual: The file appears to be a semantic index of design substrates by rhetorical arc and visual attention; its content matches that purpose.
apparent_themes: Compression levels of the same design language
Consistency of brand grammar across artefacts
intention_signals: It seems to be trying to make the implicit narrative and visual structure of design artefacts explicit, so they can be compared, queried, and reused as a shared design-language substrate.
novelty: It treats pitch-deck slide titles as a single authored sentence and describes attention flow as a scripted reading path, rather than as isolated slide content.
contribution: A structured, per-artefact account of narrative arcs and attention flows that a fused system could use to compare how different decks and pages stage arguments and guide the eye.
summary_for_embedding: A JSON meta-ledger describing narrative arcs and per-slide attention flows for seven ConceptV pitch-deck and landing-page design substrates at varying compression levels.
intention_for_embedding: To create an interpretive, queryable index of rhetorical structure and visual attention choreography across design artefacts.
